title: RTInfer: Real-Time Inference of Multiple DNNs on Edge GPUs
abstract:
While edge GPUs are increasingly used for latency-critical DNN tasks, limited resources often fail to meet strict real-time (RT) requirements under concurrent workloads. 
Existing preemption and early-exit mechanisms often underutilize GPU resources through single-task queuing and sacrifice excessive accuracy during task bursts.
To address this, we propose RTInfer, a novel system that enables concurrent RT task execution while balancing throughput and accuracy.
RTInfer integrates an accuracy-calibrated lightweight variant co-optimization to generate efficient models, a memory-layout-aware scheduler to mitigate fragmentation during preemption, and an on-demand loading strategy to minimize host-to-GPU latency.
Extensive evaluations demonstrate that RTInfer outperforms state-of-the-art methods by reducing average deadline miss rate (DMR) from 32.8\% to 0\% and improving accuracy by up to 56.5\%.
decision_comment:
This paper proposed a new co-design system for real-time DNN inference on edge GPUs, called RTInfer. It employs Accuracy-Calibrated Variant Co-Optimization, i.e., co-optimizing DNN pruning ratios and early-exits, producing a pool of DNN building blocks with different accuracy and resource trade-offs for real-time task scheduling. The optimum DNN building block selections under different constraints, including accuracy, latency, and memory, are pre-computed offline. In online scheduling, RTInfer uses a memory-layout-aware scheduler, with an ILP heuristic solver, to selects the best combination of DNN building blocks from the pools and packs them into the available 2D Time x Address memory space. Additionally, to save memory, different DNN building blocks are loaded on demand, instead of pre-loading all the building blocks into the GPU memory. The paper showed that RTInfer can achieve much lower task deadline miss rate (DMR) and higher task accuracy compared to the existing SOTA pre-emptive scheduling approaches.

Effectively and efficiently using GPU resources is a difficult problem, not to mention in the real-time setting. The co-design idea proposed in this paper is trying to solve this difficult problem. The reviewers found the proposed method elegant and interesting and the experiment results are quite impressive. Despite one of the reviewers did not vote for acceptance, I would like to try recommending this paper for acceptance anyway, because I found the authors' response to be satisfactory, and the reviewer's confidence was low.

Finally, I personally have some minor suggestions. This system heavily assume that the DNN tasks are constrained by memory but not SMs. This means, if the DNN compute work loads are high or the DNN CUDA kernel grid size scales as the problem size increases, this system might not be too effective. Adding another layer of SM level scheduling can be complicated and difficult, but the paper at least has to discuss the limitations of the new system.

reviews.summary: RTInfer is a scheduling system designed to enable the concurrent execution of multiple real-time Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) on resource-constrained edge GPUs, abandoning the inefficient serial execution queues of prior state-of-the-art systems. To achieve this parallelism without exhausting memory or missing strict deadlines, RTInfer utilizes an offline accuracy-calibrated co-optimization process to generate highly compressed model variants via pruning and early-exits. At runtime, it employs a rapid heuristic-driven Integer Linear Program (ILP) to physically pack these models into a 2D memory layout (Time × Address) without fragmentation, while a "Delta-Graph" pipeline minimizes host-to-device transfer latencies by only loading missing weight chunks when dynamically swapping variants. When evaluated on an NVIDIA Jetson edge GPU, this coordinated approach allowed RTInfer to reduce Deadline Miss Rates by up to 98.2% and improve inference accuracy by up to 58.0% during heavy task bursts compared to baseline schedulers.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses: The problem of the paper is a classical 2D-strip packing scheduling, and the practical ILP-based solution with relaxed constraints through lighter model variants provides an elegant solution. The presentation of the paper is also solid, as the problem statements are straightforward and the techniques are easy to understand. However, the paper has a significant red flag in its evaluations - it is using CNN models that are over or near 10 years old. This makes the results and thus the real-world significance of the paper hard to accurately evaluate, as the said models are now largely replaced by newer CNN models or vision transformer-based models.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
Hello, thank you for your submission! I have also worked on mobile machine learning systems, and it is good to see another mobile DNN workload scheduling paper extending the contributions of BAND, CoDL, Pantheon, etc. I believe RTInfer's ILP-based solution with relaxed constraints through lighter model variants to the 2D strip-packing scheduling problem is an elegant solution. I would love to see this paper published, but I must address the elephant in the room.

1. Why are the evaluations solely based on old CNN models? I understand that the mobile DNN workload scheduling papers traditionally focused on vision tasks using CNNs. However, the CNN models used in the paper are now 8 to 12 years old. Will it be possible to update the framework to support modern CNN models, such as YOLO26, or newer architectures, such as vision transformers?

2. How will this 2D strip packing solution work with DNN models with dynamic memory requirements, such as generative NLP models that use KV cache?
reviews.limitations: As stated in the Strengths and Weaknesses section, the main limitation of the paper is the evaluation on outdated and limited variants of DNN models. This makes the real-world significance of the paper hard to accurately evaluate, as the said models are now largely replaced by newer CNN models or vision transformer-based models, and the evaluation is also missing NLP workloads that are common in mobile environments. The authors did include a discussion on the supported models in the appendix, but the large-scale study that the authors cited to justify the sole use of CNNs is also 7 years old. I am willing to increase my score if the evaluation is updated with newer CNN models and non-vision models, such as generative NLP models with dynamic memory uses (e.g., KV cache).

reviews.summary:
RTInfer seems to be a system that is designed to enable concurrent real-time DNN inference on resource-constrained edge devices. The paper begins by stating that pre-exsting approaches with preemption and early-exit mechanisms often underutilize GPU resources ro even sacrifice accuracy.

To complement this, the paper proposes three-part architecture that comprises RTInfer: Accuracy-Calibrated Variant Co-Optimization which is an offline process that uses generic algorithm to generate a pool of model variants through pruning and early-exit insertion. These variants are fine-tuned to maintain high accuracy even with reduced memory footprints; (2) Memory-Layout-Aware Task Scheduling: is an online scheduler that jointly optimizes task priority and spatial memory layout to prevent fragmentation while using ILP to ensure concurrent tasks fit within the limited GPU memory budget; (3) Delta-Graph and Load-Aware Pipeline which is a mechanism that minimizes host-to-GPU transfer latency by prefetching only specific chunks needed for a variant switch.

Evaluation on NVIDIA Jetson Xavier NX seems to demonstrate that RTInfer reduces the deadline miss rate by up to 98.2 and improves accuracy by up to 58.0%.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
* Joint optimization of memory layout, latency, and accuracy seems interesting.
* Memory-layout-aware scheduling seems to reduce GPU memory fragmentation.
* Delta-Graph approach seems to reduce the overhead of switching between multiple DNN variants.

Weaknesses:
* Use of GA for offline variant selection and ILP for online scheduling may introduce complexity and may be costly.
* Delta-Graph seems to put big restriction as it requires models to share a common backbone.
* Seems to be specific to NVIDIA Jetson GPUs. What about NPUs? What about other types of GPUs?
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
* While the paper seems to report 58% improvement in accuracy, it seems to stem from assigning an accuracy of 0% to tasks that miss the deadlines. It would be nice to understand the accuracy if you exclude deadline misses and only compare the accuracy of completed tasks.
* Pantheon seems to be designed specifically for serial execution. It would provide more insight if it was compared to more modern concurrent GPU schedulers or spatial-sharing frameworks such as REEF?
* It would be great to understand the impact of ILP solver latency. While the paper seems to suggest that it utilizes ILP for online scheduling, it is commonly thought to be an expensive algorithm to run for such case? Can you present average and worst-case execution time of the scheduler?
* Regarding "memory-layout-aware" scheduling, how does RTInfer handle the non-deterministic memory management of the underlying NVIDIA driver? Users typically do not have low-level control over how the GPU driver allocates physical memory blocks, how do you guarantee that your "layout" is actually respected by the hardware?
* How would you generalize the ideal of Delta-Graph to cases where it is difficult to make it share a common backbone. Wouldn't there be no gain from this approach? Is there a way to complement this?

reviews.summary: This paper studies methods for optimizing DNN workloads in real-time systems. The main ideas are: (1) applying early exit while jointly considering accuracy and timing constraints, and designing the system to find solutions that satisfy required accuracy levels; (2) designing runtime scheduling to minimize memory fragmentation; and (3) incorporating CPU–GPU communication considerations into the scheduling strategy. The implementation shows better performance than the baseline Pantheon, achieving lower DMR and higher accuracy.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
== Strengths ==

The paper clearly describes the implementation details. The problem motivation and the proposed solutions are well aligned and easy to understand. In particular, Figure 4 clearly illustrates the difference between the previous SOTA approach and the proposed method.

The overall performance results are strong. The proposed method achieves improvements in both DMR and accuracy.

The evaluation section is also quite comprehensive. The ablation studies are reasonably thorough.

== Weaknesses ==

The concerns are closer to questions rather than clear weaknesses. The following points were not fully clarified while reading the paper.

First, it is unclear how the boundary between offline tuning and online scheduling is determined. In a runtime system, the exact workload pattern may not be known in advance. The paper performs task-level optimization using techniques such as genetic algorithms during the offline phase, but it is not clear how these optimizations remain effective when the actual mixture of tasks at runtime may vary. Does the system perform the search for all possible mixtures of tasks in advance? If so, it is unclear whether this assumption is reasonable. In general, it would help to clarify what range of task mixtures the system assumes during deployment, and whether this assumption is realistic.

Second, there is some uncertainty about the choice of the DMR baseline. If previous methods show fault ratios close to 100%, it suggests that those methods may already be impractical in this setting, independent of the proposed approach. It would be helpful to understand how prior work interprets these results, and whether the experimental conditions used in this paper might be unusually harsh.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
I am interested in the questions raised in the weaknesses section and would appreciate clarification from the authors. 
Separately, Figure 4 provides a clear illustration of the scheduling differences between the baseline and the proposed approach. Would it be possible to provide similar scheduling descriptions for other previous methods, such as RMS-P and DMS-P?

reviews.summary: This paper presents RTInfer, a system designed to address challenges when multiple priority-based tasks share edge GPU resources. The key problems are: (1) limited GPU memory and fragmentation caused by poor task scheduling, (2) accuracy loss from model pruning, and (3) H2D transfer latency that bottlenecks model offloading. RTInfer proposes three solutions: Accuracy-Calibrated Variant Co-Optimization to handle pruning-accuracy tradeoffs, memory-layout-aware scheduling to reduce fragmentation, and Delta-Graph with Load-Aware Pipeline to minimize transfer overhead.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths
+ Identifies memory fragmentation caused by improper task scheduling.
+ Provides a clear mathematical model that captures both time and memory dimensions in GPU task multiplexing.

Weakness
- The memory constraint appears artificially created rather than naturally present in the listed edge workloads.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
Thanks for your submission to ICML. 
I appreciate the high-level approach: scheduling multiple real-time tasks to share GPU resources, using scheduling policies that reduce memory fragmentation, and applying pruning methods to lower memory usage. These techniques should be particularly effective in the era of large models, which demand significantly more GPU memory.

However, this paper has one critical issue as indicated in the weakness above. Please address Q1 below in detail, and I will reconsider my recommendation positively if you can clarify this concern.

Q1. Memory constraint justification (Response to weakness)
The memory requirements claimed in the motivation and solution do not match the actual edge workloads listed in this paper. I calculated the peak memory usage for all models in Table 1 using FP32 precision, batch size 16 (it is always 1 in RT inference), and standard input tensors (bs, 3, 224, 224) under CUDA 12.5 and PyTorch 2.4. The total peak memory calculated for all the models is approximately 5GB, which does not exceed the 8GB or 16GB available on Jetson Xavier NX.
I notice you increased certain parameters in Section 6.1 (paragraph 2), which appears to artificially create a memory-constrained scenario that supports your motivation. However, in a realistic edge-GPU setting with CNN-based models, the limited memory problem you describe does not naturally exist. Please clarify why these parameter adjustments are necessary and representative, or justify why the memory constraint is a real problem in practical edge-GPU scenarios.

Q2. Evaluation on larger models (Suggestion for future work)
Based on my earlier comments, RTInfer should be particularly effective for memory-constrained scenarios, such as deploying large models on edge devices. While this would strengthen the paper, adding extensive new experiments in this rebuttal cycle cannot address the core issue raised in Q1. I recommend focusing on clarifying Q1 first.

Minor
Writing typos: Missing spaces after "RTInfer" (page 2 paragraph 1, conclusion section); page 1 right side wrong citation; unclear section labels in Figure 5.
